Chapter four - Research Methodology 
This chapter gives the rationale for the research methodology, and research method chosen 
for this project.  Key challenges encountered and ethical considerations are also discussed here.  
IV.1 Selecting an appropriate research methodology 
IV.1.1 
Impact of my worldview on the selection process 
As a Christian, I believe that God created all and declared that creation was good 
per Gen 1:31 “God saw all that he had made, and it was very good…”(The Holy Bible, 1984).  
Although we are made in the image of God (Gen 1:27 So God created mankind in his own 
image), each is fearfully and wonderfully made (Psalm 139:14), i.e. each one of us is custom-
made.  Each individual is unique and deserves to be studied as such.  This is aptly expressed 
by Gerkin in his book on human beings as living human document: “each individual living 
document has an integrity of his or her own that calls for understanding and interpretation, 
not categorization and stereotyping” (Gerkin, 1984, p.38).  Statistics from a quantitative 
methodology can be used to provide a general overview of the state of affairs and I would 
use them for that purpose, but I do not think they represent a person well and is not 
appropriate for a research project to understand the spirituality of OAs.  
Further, since God created everything and human beings are made in the image of 
God (Gen 1:27), it should be possible to learn more about the living God and His purpose 
through them - just as we can learn about Him through the life stories, occurrences etc. in 
the Bible (Bennett et al., 2018, p.30).  Knowledge is gained through experiences, both my 
own and those of others – in life experiences, observations of creation, innovations, analyses, 
 
 
  
VERSION_DEC 2025 
 
 
73 
 
reflections etc. and experiences are best understood if they are studied in context.  To be 
contextual, it is necessary to understand the experiences shared by the OAs through their 
interpretive framework and be mindful of how my own interpretative framework impacts 
my understanding of their experiences.  Hence, between qualitative and quantitative research 
methodology, my personal preference is the former but I arrived at the final decision to use 
a qualitative methodology because of the purpose of this research project.  
IV.1.2 
Reasons for a qualitative methodological approach for this project 
My purpose of carrying out this research project is to gain insight to the relationship 
between the spirituality of OAs and their experience of old age.  I seek more to understand 
and not so much to explain.  Bearing in mind this purpose, the research question and 
assumptions, I concluded that the most appropriate research methodology for this project is 
a flexible or qualitative research design.   
Fixed or quantitative research designs usually involve the use of aggregates and 
working with group properties and general tendencies.  They are theory-driven and usually 
designed to avoid any “experimenter effect” (Robson, 2002, p.98).  My research was a small-
scale project which did not involve the use of aggregates or trends.  It was not undertaken to 
prove or disprove a particular hypothesis.  For this project, I was not using a well-developed 
theory with identified variables that could be used to design procedures and create a 
somewhat controlled environment to test these variables.  As a practical theologian working 
with OAs to understand their spirituality and its role in their lives, I needed a research design 
that would enable me to work closely with the research participants instead of being a 
 
 
  
VERSION_DEC 2025 
 
 
74 
 
“detached” researcher.  That would mean taking account of my own role in the research as 
a research-practitioner, but there was not enough information to design a fixed/quantitative 
research plan that could isolate the “experimenter effect”.  Instead, by using a 
flexible/qualitative research approach, intentional practices of reflexivity could be relied on 
to manage the effects of any researcher bias. 
A flexible research design allows for an evolving design, the presentation of 
multiple perspectives, the use of the researcher as an instrument of data collection and a 
focus on the participants’ views.  It is just as rigorous as a fixed research design, uses 
combination of methods for data collection and the data collected can be analysed at different 
levels of abstraction (Robson, 2002, p.166).  The findings can be reported as narratives which 
reflect the realities of life more accurately than averages or trends.  
The flexible research methodological framework that I chose is Interpretative 
Phenomenological Analysis (IPA).  The primary goal of IPA research is to investigate how 
individuals make sense of their experiences (Pietkiewicz, 2012).  It is based on 
phenomenology, pioneered by Husserl and further developed by Heidegger, Merleau-Ponty 
and Sartre (Smith, Flowers and Larkin, 2009).   It synthesises ideas from phenomenology 
and hermeneutics to produce a methodology that is both descriptive and interpretative.  
According to Larkin and Thompson (2011, p.102), “IPA has an interpretative 
phenomenological epistemology….It assumes that understanding the world requires 
understanding of experiences” and the research participants are always “immersed in a 
linguistic, relational, cultural and physical world…therefore the IPA researcher needs to take 
 
 
  
VERSION_DEC 2025 
 
 
75 
 
an idiographic approach…to facilitate a detailed focus on the particular”.  There is a dual 
interpretation process because research participant makes sense of his/her experience and 
then the IPA researcher tries to decode that meaning, i.e. makes sense of the participant’s 
meaning-making.  To make sense of the others’ experiences, the IPA researcher needs to be 
able to identify and reflect on her own experiences and assumptions.  The final assumption 
is that researchers “cannot escape interpretation at any stage but can reflect on their role in 
producing those interpretations and maintain a commitment to ground them in the research 
participants’ views” (Larkin and Thompson, 2011, p.103).  These assumptions are 
reasonable and useful and I tried to keep them in mind throughout the research project.  
The main concern of IPA is to give full appreciation of each research participant’s 
account as IPA studies have small sample sizes and usually involve verbatim transcripts of 
first-person accounts that have been generated by a particular research participant.  This 
fitted my intention of giving full attention to each of my research participants so that I could 
understand their context and the experiences they share as well as I could.  
Smith, Flowers and Larkin (2009) identified six steps in using IPA.  The first step 
involves reading and re-reading the transcript of the interview to enter into the research 
participant’s world.  This allows the researcher to construct an overall structure of the 
interview and discern any patterns e.g. from generic to specifics as rapport between the 
researcher and the participant is built up during the interview.  The second step, initial noting, 
is where the researcher maintains an open mind and writes any explanatory notes or 
comments as she reads the transcript, examining the semantic content and language used.  
 
 
  
VERSION_DEC 2025 
 
 
76 
 
Developing from such descriptive comments that stay close to the participant’s explicit 
meaning, the researcher is also expected to do more interpretative noting to understand how 
and why the participant may have certain concerns.  The authors wrote that “it is important 
to engage in analytic dialogue with each line of transcript, asking questions of what the word, 
phrase, sentence means to the researcher and attempting to check what it means to the 
participant” (Smith, Flowers and Larkin, 2009, p.84).  The third step of developing emergent 
themes involves working primarily with the initial notes after having a very familiar model 
of the interview.  The narrative flow of the interview is broken and discrete parts of the 
transcript are used to support the identified themes.  This step of the analysis involves more 
of the researcher in the sense that the research participant provided the original words to 
support the themes, but the interpretation belongs to the analyst.  The fourth step involves 
searching for connections across emergent themes. The emergent themes are mapped 
according to how the analyst thinks they fit together bearing in mind the research question. 
This is where super-ordinate themes emerge.  The fifth step is to move to the next case, 
treating it like it is the first and allowing new themes to emerge.  This is in line with the 
idiographic commitment of IPA.  The sixth and last step is to look for patterns across cases. 
This is done with the help of a master table of themes for the research project.  This 
comparison would lead to reconfiguration or relabeling of the themes.  It could also help the 
analysis move to a more theoretical level if it is recognized that themes that is particular to 
an individual case are also shared by other cases.

From the above description, one can see that the IPA methodology calls for a 
meticulous, rigorous and time-consuming research process.  Although it was developed for 
use in research in psychology, I believe that it is a research methodology that is suitable for 
my interdisciplinary project, capable of producing insightful and rich accounts of the 
research participants’ experiences that would give insight into their spirituality and how it 
supported them in old age.  
IV.2 The research method 
My research question is “What is the spirituality of Christian Chinese-Singaporean older 
adults who are positively engaged with life?”   The question is open and exploratory as I seek to 
understand their relationship with God, the experiences that shaped their spirituality, their 
meanings of such experiences, their responses to such meanings and the effects, if any, on their 
experience of old age.   
The research participants were selected purposively, i.e. six OAs who met the selection 
criteria (see section III.4).  According to Smith, Flowers and Larkin (2009, p.51), many studies by 
experienced IPA researchers have sample sizes in the range of three to six.  Hence, a sample size 
of six should be enough to provide for the development of meaningful points of difference and 
similarity among them and not generate too much data to jeopardize IPA’s commitment to good 
qualitative research work.  
The six research participants are 65 years or older Singaporeans who are community 
dwelling and not in institutions such as nursing homes or homes for the aged.  The chronological 
age of 65 was used as the minimum age because it is the most commonly used age for defining 
 
 
  
VERSION_DEC 2025 
 
 
78 
 
OAs in Singapore, making it easier to compare this research results against other local research 
results.  One reason for selecting community dwelling OAs was because the research participants 
needed to be healthy enough to attend a one-to-one interview session lasting at least an hour.  The 
other reason is because I work with such community-dwelling OAs in my practice.  
The research participants were supposed to be invited from the various eldercare centers 
that I worked with and the center managers had agreed to be my gatekeepers to ensure that my 
interaction with the research participants were respectful and cordial.  However, this process was 
disrupted by the Covid-19 pandemic where the government ordered all the eldercare facilities for 
community-dwelling OAs to be closed.   In the end, one of the center managers helped me recruit 
those OAs that volunteered at her center instead of the center attendees.  These volunteers met my 
selection criteria but they were all Chinese.  This is not surprising because 85.4% of the local 
Christian population is Chinese according to the 2020 population census(Department of Statistics 
Singapore, 2021).  With such a sample, ethnicity is clearly not a research variable and my research 
question was modified to reflect this. 
 During the recruitment process, potential participants were given information on the 
research project including the research method, i.e. interviews, the time commitment, the 
discussion topic, the proposed interview dates and the type of protection that would be accorded 
to the data that they shared.  Only those OAs who could and were prepared to sign written consent 
forms were invited to participate in the project.  They were  prepared to talk about their 
understanding of spirituality, those experiences that they considered spiritual and how such 
experiences had impacted them and their outlook to life.  Their gender and ethnicity were not of

concern to me initially since I was not looking for a sample that was representative of Singapore’s 
Christian population.  Post my annual review with the university review board, the ratio of male 
to female participants was changed to 1:2 so that there was more male participants in my sample.   
To have rich, first-person, detailed accounts of the research participants’ experiences, given 
my lack of experience with groups, I chose one-to-one in-depth interviews as my data collection 
method although some IPA research projects were done with focus groups.  In the one-to-one 
interviews, I was able to give full attention to each research participant and had more reaction time 
for good quality interaction with him/her. 
Being new to the IPA methodology, I used semi-structured interviews so I had a list of  
questions (see Annex A) to remind me of the topics that should be covered, but also had room for 
the research participants to explore areas that I had not thought of.  The questions related mainly 
to experiences that had given them meaning and purpose in life, important relationships especially 
their relationship with God and how they felt those experiences had impacted how they perceived 
and lived their lives etc.    The questions were open-ended, and their use subjected to the pace and 
the flow of the conversation.  As the research focus was on the spirituality of the participants, the 
discussions were intimate and sensitive at times.  Respect for the participants overrode the 
interview schedules.  Effects of the interview on the participant were monitored, including non-
verbal cues and the interview pace was adjusted to take them into account.  The sequence of the 
questions and number of questions asked were also adjusted as the interviews progressed.  The 
target time of the interview was one and a half hour and I tried to cap it to the maximum of two 
hours, but it was difficult at times to interrupt the flow of the participant’s thoughts and feelings

unless they felt comfortable about retaining those parts after such discussions. 
Two pilot interviews were conducted with my friends so that the interview questions could 
be tested and amended before using them with the selected research participants.  These interviews

During the interviews, I tried to take some notes but realised that it was not possible as I

participant but realized that it was not necessary as the original interviews were already very rich. 
IV.3 Language considerations 
In Singapore, most OAs are likely to be more comfortable in their mother tongues than 
English because most of them would have picked up English only when they entered school or the 
workforce.  According to government statistics, the group of local OAs who were my potential 
participants have, on average, 4.7 years of education (Department of Statistics Singapore, 2019, 
p.vi).  As such, the research participants were asked beforehand if they were comfortable to be 
interviewed in English.  One of the participants preferred not to be interviewed in English, so her 
interview was conducted in Mandarin.  For that one interview, the transcript was translated into 
English before analysis.  
 
 
  
VERSION_DEC 2025

Another issue that I foresaw, based on my interaction with local OAs was that, even those 
who were fluent in English switched to their mother tongues during conversations when they could 
not readily find the English words or phrases that could describe the meanings that they wanted to 
convey.  For instance, many used idioms in their mother tongues or colloquial phrases such as 
those in Singlish.  This is could be because they felt that certain nuances their dialects or Singlish 
convey would be lost when translated into English, or even Mandarin.  Unfortunately, both 
interpretation and translation involve an element of interpretation and some analysis by the 
interpreter/translator.  As such, in the English transcripts, both the words or phrases in their original 
language/dialects and their translations were shown.  In this paper, key phrases or words quoted 
are included in their original language/dialects as well as their translations into English to inform 
the readers of this additional process (Marshall and Rossman, 2010, p.166).  A copy of the 
interview transcripts were also offered to the respective research participants for feedback to help 
correct any errors in the transcripts.  
IV.4 Ethical considerations 
My key ethical consideration is to maintain the appropriate attitude throughout this 
research project, i.e. not to impose or be seen to impose my beliefs on my research participants 
either during the interview or when analysing the transcripts.  This awareness was always at the 
back of my mind throughout this project.  My research participants are OAs and they are a 
vulnerable group.  For example, they are more likely to suffer from some form of health 
impairments as compared to young adults e.g. hard of hearing, being forgetful at times, or tires 
easily.  As such, they could be bullied or deceived into acting to their detriment or giving up their

or have not been keeping themselves up to date.  For instance, they may not be aware of their rights 
under newer legislations such as the Personal Data Protection Act (PDPA) in Singapore.  As a 
researcher it is my personal responsibility to protect my research participants and not abuse their 
trust.  One safeguard in place was the center manager since I recruited my research participants 
through her.  
During the recruitment of research participants, I also explained my research project, the 
information that I would be gathering from them and the time commitment.  They were encouraged 
to ask questions before committing to the project.  Informed consent was sought from each 
participant respectfully, and their consent were all in written form so they could read what they 
were consenting to.  The informed consent form included consent: to participate in the project; to 
report illegal activities, for instance, if there were clear signs of elder abuse; to do a voice recording 
of our interviews; and to allow parts of the information that they shared to be made public, for 
example, verbatim extracts published in journals or presentation at conferences.  Their privacy 
were protected through the shield of anonymity, i.e. their names were replaced with pseudonyms, 
i.e. research participants A, B etc.  Even with such consent, I will not publish such information if 
it is so unique that the research participant can be readily identified.  The research participants 
were informed that they could withdraw from the research project any time during the data 
collection phase if they felt uncomfortable.  This right to withdraw is time limited though, i.e. 
within one month of the interview (Smith, Flowers and Larkin, 2009).  Before signing the written 
consent, the form was explained and the research participant were reminded that if there were

V.3 Transcribing the interviews 
V.3.1 
The transcription process 
I tried using auto-transcription software, i.e. “Descript” and “Express Scribe”  to 
transcribe the recorded interviews but the accuracy rate was low.  There were several reasons 
for this: the accents of the research participants; the use of multiple languages and dialects 
in the same recording; there were also background noises in some of the recordings; 
variations in the volume of parts of the recordings as the participants leaned forward or 
moved away from the voice recorder; and the fast pace at which some participants spoke.  
The lowest automatic transcription rate came from the interview conducted in Mandarin 
because our heavily accented local Mandarin was not readily understood by software 
developed using standard Mandarin. 
Consequently, all the interview recordings were transcribed manually. When 
manually transcribed, I could transcribe close to 100% of the conversations except for a few 
words where loud external noises, such as clanking pots/pans at the hawker centre, obscured

The coding method chosen for this research is in vivo coding because it allows the

them subsequently would result in a lot of work.  In NVivo, it was also easy to create a

was made easier.  The software also allowed me to choose if I wanted to “aggregate” the 
references from the “child” codes up to the “parent” code.

V.5 Recording the impact of the Covid-19 pandemic 
The first case of Covid-19 was detected in Singapore on 23 Jan 2020 and first set of “circuit 
breaker” restrictions, i.e. was imposed from 7 Apr to 4 May which included shutting down all non-
essential workplaces including eldercare centers, schools etc.  Both private and public gatherings 
were not allowed.  These restrictions were extended to 1 Jun 2020, then there were progressive 
loosening of the restrictions until Covid-19 vaccinations became available from 30 Dec 2020.  Life 
returned almost to normal after most of the population was vaccinated and most restrictions were 
lifted on 26 Apr 2022 (Goh, 2022).  During circuit-breaker periods, there was much fear as there 
was insufficient information about Covid-19 and deaths were reported every day in the media.  To 
capture the impact of Covid-19 on the research participants, I decided to add  two questions about 
the pandemic to my list of interview questions (see Annex A).   
V.6 Conclusion 
Due to the Covid-19 restrictions which shut down all the eldercare centers, the participants 
who were recruited for this project were all OAs that had volunteered at one of the eldercare centers 
that had agreed to participate in this research instead of its programme participants.  Four research 
participants were interviewed in person and two through video calls.  All transcripts were prepared 
manually including the Chinese transcript that required translation.  In vivo and concept coding 
were used to code the transcripts.  A variety of software were used to do the coding, i.e. Excel 
spreadsheets, the software NVivo and Word tables.  The data gathering and coding process took

VI. 
Chapter six – Findings 
This chapter explains the themes arising from the coding process and how they were 
identified.  It explains the process of coding and categorizing with examples to illustrate the 
process; as well as the categories and the patterns or themes that emerged. 
VI.1 Details about coding the transcripts 
Several rounds of coding were done on the six interview transcripts, each with a different 
focus.  During the first round of coding, each transcript was read to code the participant’s faith 
journey – starting from their family of origin, introduction to Christianity, conversion, and finally 
their current state of spirituality.  In vivo quotes explaining the context of each of their research 
participant were extracted and coded.  As the impact of Covid-19 was an area of special research, 
direct quotes were extracted to evidence the participants’ interpretation of this phenomenon.  This 
first attempt generated a lot of codes, but the coding, re-coding and generation of several code 
structures gave me a better understanding of the historical and current context of the participants.  
The next round of coding was focused on capturing each participant’s understanding what 
being a Christian meant to them and how it had impacted their engagement with life.  In vivo and 
concept coding (Saldaña, 2016, p.97) were done.  Spreadsheets were used in the first round and 
NVivo was used in both rounds of coding.   
A third round of coding was done using only Microsoft WORD tables.  The intention was 
to look anew at the transcripts to learn as much as possible about the participants and ensure the 
codes produced were as consistent as possible with the participants’ meaning.  The WORD tables 
originally consisted of three columns including 
 
 
  
VERSION_DEC 2025 
 
 
99 
 
- 
Direct quotes from the transcripts; 
- 
Exploratory comments (including descriptive, linguistic and concept comments); and  
- 
Emergent themes. 
They were modelled  after the table in Jonathan Smith’s book on IPA (Smith, Flowers and 
Larkin, 2009, p.84).  Smith is the main author who introduced the IPA research methodology.   
However, while analysing the transcripts, I realised that using the tables as they were, made 
me to jump from codes to emergent themes without explaining the process.  Hence, I modified the 
tables so that the last column was changed from “Emergent themes” to “Categories”. Saldaña 
(2016, pp.10) had called this transition from coding to categorising “synthesis”, i.e. the move 
towards consolidated meaning.  Given the large number of codes generated, consolidating the 
codes into categories according to their meanings, made it easy for me to see any patterns emerging 
amongst the categories, something which was easier than looking for patterns amongst codes.  
These patterns can then be analysed to see if they occurred across transcripts, i.e. if they are group 
themes.  An example of these working tables is presented in section VI.3 which explains the actual 
coding and categorising process.

VI.2.1 
Participant A 
Participant A was born in Johore Bahru (JB), a city in the Malaysian State that is 
closest to Singapore, to a wealthy Chinese Christian family. She was the only second-
generation Christian among the six research participants and she proudly proclaimed that 
“down the line, every generation that I looked there is either a pastor or missionary”.  
Unfortunately, during World War II (WWII), her father was taken away by the Japanese 
when he tried to retrieve the body of her grandfather who had been shot by the invading 
force.  Her mother inherited a large part of her father’s legacy but could not get along with 
participant A’s paternal grandmother.   
Participant A’s mother returned to her hometown in Singapore with her son and 
four daughters.  She was then cheated of most of her husband’s legacy and felt that she could 
no longer bring up her five children.  Participant A’s brother was returned to their 
grandmother because participant A said her grandmother “wanted” him.  As the son of her 
eldest boy, there was a plot of land in JB, their hometown for him to inherit, but she did not 
want the four granddaughters.  So, participant A’s mother sent participant A and her sisters 
to a welfare home which is in another Malaysian state, Malacca.  Subsequently, the girls 
encountered “some unpleasantness” in the home and one of participant A’s sister “got sick”, 
so the girls returned to their mother in Singapore.  They stayed with their mother in her 
rented room but had to find food for themselves during the day.  The girls picked up 
vegetables from the nearby wet market to cook in the communal kitchen.  Participant A 
described the poor relations among the neighbours renting rooms in the building: “If the

complain, you just have to live together like that”.  The girls were also expected to support 
themselves and their brother “when we grew up, we have to, give him the money.  My mom 
have to take money from us.  How we earn and what we do, huh, it's our own.  The girls,

Throughout her struggles to survive, participant A held on to her Christian faith.

VI.2.2 
Participant B 
Participant B was born near the end of WWII in a Singaporean Chinese family and 
had a secondary school education.  As a teenager, one of his secondary school teachers took 
him to Sunday service but he did not become a Christian until “at the age of 38, my brother 
brought me to Christ”.  His brother studied theology and was involved in mission work in 
the Philippines.  Participant B said that he was a “strong Buddhist” before he became a 
Christian and he participated in local religious rites and festivals such as the Hungry Ghost 
festival.  These practices are not the typical practices of canonical Buddhists, i.e. Buddhists 
that followed the teachings of the two main schools of Buddhism in Singapore, namely 
Theravada and Mahayana Buddhism.  What participant B had described were practices of 
the main Chinese folk religion in Singapore, Shenism.    
 
 
  
VERSION_DEC 2025 
 
 
102 
 
Among local Chinese OAs, the term “Buddhism” often refers to “the Chinese 
syncretic religions practised in Singapore” as pointed out by the local anthropologist, 
Vivienne Wee, an associate faculty of the Singapore University of Social Sciences.  Wee 
also wrote that “of the Chinese syncretic religions, the one most frequently encountered is 
Shenism.  In fact, one may even say that the majority of Singaporeans who call themselves 
‘Buddhists’ are in fact Shenist” (Wee, 1976, p.169,170).  Shenism is a term coined by the 
British anthropologist Alan J. A. Elliot to describe the syncretic Chinese religion practised 
by the local Chinese which draws its themes from “popular and corrupt Taoism, with 
influences from Confucianism and Buddhism and, perhaps, more importantly, from ancient 
folk religion” (Elliott, 1955, p.29).  Elliott visited Singapore as part of his research on the 
cults in the country between 1951-52.  Most local Chinese in the 1950s practised 拜拜 
(pronounced as BaiBai) or 拜神 (pronounced as BaiShen).  “Bai” 拜 literally means to pray 
to, and “Shen” 神 means gods or deities, where “Shen” is “a major category among the 
powerful and fearful spiritual beings which have to be worshipped in order to secure human 
wellbeing” (Elliott, 1955, p.28).  Therefore, Shenism is polytheistic and the “Shens” are able 
to communicate with their followers through dreams, spirit-mediums or the use of divination 
blocks 筊杯 (pronounced as Jiaobei), a pair of crescent shaped blocks (Wee, 1976, p.172).   
The term “Shenism” is not a commonly used term among the locals and it has also 
not been listed as a religion option that one can choose from when the local government 
departments collected statistics on the religious affiliations of Singaporeans, for example, 
during its population censuses.  Hence, the local Shenists usually select either “Buddhism” 
 
 
  
VERSION_DEC 2025 
 
 
103 
 
or “Taoism*” as their religion during the population census, with the asterisk on the “Taoism” 
option indicating that it includes Chinese traditional beliefs (Department of Statistics 
Singapore, 2021, p.31).  This explains why participant B used the Buddhist and Taoist 
interchangeably.  At the beginning of our interview, participant B said: “before I was a very 
strong Buddhist lor.  My wife also got shocked that I became a Christian”, but later in our 
conversation, he said: “the Lord guide me lah because before that, before that I was Taoist”.   
Participant B did not experience a complete transformation of his lifestyle 
immediately after he accepted Jesus as his saviour, it was a gradual process of change.  
During this period, he had important spiritual experiences that helped strengthened his faith.  
He is an active member of his church and after retiring from full time employment, he 
volunteered with several church outreach programmes.

Conversion experiences “involve a major change in a person’s thinking, feeling and willing 
– in short, in their total behaviour” (Westerhoff, 2000, p.94).  Participant A was envious of 
her husband because he studied the faith before converting to Christianity so he was able to 
live as a Christian wholeheartedly after conversion.  On the other hand, she carried the 
Christian label from birth but spent many years searching her faith.  She compared the faith 
journey of Christian converts like her husband and her faith journey, saying: “So they know 
from reading the Bible now they know what it is life is (they learnt their life purpose).  So 
they do everything.  For us, since born leh, I don't have that.  Only the years He bring us.”  
Her husband was a Buddhist when they got married but he “embraced Christianity” after 
studying the Bible and receiving God’s calling.   Despite being born in a Christian family 
and attending church service from young, participant A said that she and her siblings were 
“very happy” to attend church on Sundays “not for the service ah, because as children we 
wear new clothes.  So, you just waited for Sunday where you have new clothes, you know, 
wear nice clothing to church”.  After the tragic events happened in her family, she went 
through a long phase of being critical: “….like myself to float around from one place to 
another place to hear the sermon but you doesn’t like it, you can go back and say: aiya, the 
sermon or the church is no good”.  She visited church after church and “was looking for 
years”.   She admitted that she could not “go all out for it (her faith)” because she was not 
“ready” whereas her husband was able to live the Christian life once he decided to become 
a Christian.

As mentioned above in the background information of the participants (see section 
VI.2 above), only the participant D experienced an immediate conversion – her centers of 
value, images of power and master story changed, i.e. contents of her faith (Fowler, 1995, 
pp.281–282) which in turn changed how she lived.  Her change was obvious enough to be 
noted by her family, triggering their attempts to coerce her to renounce Christianity (see 
section VI.5.5 below for details).  The rest of the participants took some time for the contents 
of their faith to change and to apply that to their daily living.  
The second finding was that the five research participants who were Christian 
converts were introduced to Christianity when they were very young, i.e. in their teens, either 
through the school, teachers or fellow students.  
The third finding was that all the research participants had exposure to Shenism (see 
section VI.2.2 above for more information on Shenism) including participant A, the second-
generation Christian.  The key “conflicting” belief between Shenism and Christianity 
mentioned by most of the research participants was that Christians only serve one God but 
Shenism is polytheistic with many family rituals to pray to various “Shen”.  Hence, many 
Shenists regarded those who have converted to Christianity as having rejected their lifestyles 
and in more extreme case, them.  Some of them are wary of Christians as they do not want 
their family members to become Christians.  Participant A described an incident where she 
helped a fellow patient to the ward toilet during a hospitalisation stay.  Instead of being 
thanked, participant A was “warned” by the daughters of this fellow patient against talking 
 
 
  
VERSION_DEC 2025 
 
 
117 
 
to their mother because “we are staunch Buddhists (Shenists), don't come and disturb my 
mother”.   
For two of the participants, the fears associated with certain local religious practices 
served as a push factor for them to become Christians.  Participant B talked about being 
attacked by evil spirits when he was working on an island where bomohs (Malay shamans 
and traditional medicine practitioner) prayed at random graves; and participant F described 
the fear that she felt when told by the spirit-medium that her child became sick because 
certain spirits had been offended and she had to give them offerings at a specified crossroad 
to appease them.  
In their interviews, all six also described significant life events that they felt had 
changed their lives, impacting their spiritual lives directly or indirectly.  These included the 
loss of someone dear suddenly or through a painful illness, being sent away to a welfare 
home and being threatened with death by one’s mother.  These changed the participants’ 
perspective of who they needed in life.  For some, it triggered the search for God, and others 
a renewed reliance on their Christian faith.  
Finally, the last influence coded in all six transcript was the impact of the local 
culture, especially the patriarchal attitude which had adversely impacted the lives of the 
female participants when they were young.  Interestingly, those who held such attitudes were 
the powerful female family members such as the participant A’s grandmother and the 
mothers of the participant D and F.  When participant A’s mother could not afford to upkeep

VI.4.5 
Own theological beliefs 
To live their faith, the research participants have Christian beliefs that they 
subscribe to.  Those discussed in the interviews are consolidated into this category and 
shown in the table below.  There were five beliefs that were brought up by all six research 
participants.   
VI.4.5.1 God cares 
The first belief was that God cares about them - He guides, loves, protects, 
and generally takes care of them (see the first row of table 13 below).  It is especially 
important for those participants that did not have parental love such as participant A 
and D (see section VI.2 Background information on the research participants).  
Participant A said that “If I am left to myself and I don't have this (John 3:16), I think 
I'm now nowhere” whereas  participant B said that “He loves me dearly” “the Lord 
guides me” “God is protecting me all the while”, the latter is especially important to 
him because he said: “twice I was attacked by those evil spirits before I became a 
Christian”.  Participant C said that after her husband passed away, God “became more 
real to me” “taking care of me” “the Lord console me” and “through his illness, I mean, 
the Lord really give me peace.”  Participant D, who felt “no love” from her family, 
was touched by God during a worship service and was “in love with God” since.  She 
said: “God saw how much you love Him, so whole-heartedly, so sincere, I was able to 
meet God again.  God met me repeatedly because my whole-heartedness, my 
devotedness”.  She had experienced “many miracles” and blessings after she became 
 
 
  
VERSION_DEC 2025 
 
 
126 
 
a Christian including being blessed with children “I was barren, God gave me a son”.  
Participant E believes in God’s “love for us, you know, as His children” “where the 
Bible describes God as our father, it’s an image I can associate, closely associate with” 
as participant E’s late father was a father that loved his family.  Finally, participant F 
believed that God “so love” her preserving her from the time she was in “my mother’s 
womb, when I was not a Christian till now I am a Christian, but I am so blessed. That 
in my mother’s womb You preserved me to be a Christian when I am more elderly”.  
For the participants, this belief that God cares for them is supported by their lived 
experiences, and such care is valuable to them.  
VI.4.5.2 God is in charge and His plan 
The next two beliefs mentioned by all the research participants, especially in 
connection with the then ongoing pandemic were: they believed that God was in 
charge/in control and that God had planned everything.  Participant A narrated an 
incident where her stepbrother’s attempt to sell her mother’s home without her 
permission was thwarted.  Participant A’s mother suffered from dementia and he had 
asked her to move in with his family, taking over her care.  On the day he brought an 
agent to see her flat, participant A’s mother was visiting her next-door neighbour so 
she questioned and stopped him.  Participant A said: “that’s why, on the same day you 
know.  How did my mother know that they are going to be there?”  She felt that it was 
all in God’s control.  Participant B also said: “He is God, He created everything, so He 
knows what His plan for the world” “He is in charge of everything”.  Participant C 
 
 
  
VERSION_DEC 2025 
 
 
127 
 
echoed this when she said: “God is in control.  So, leave it to Him, surrender to Him” 
whereas participant D said that “the days of our lives are in God's hands” “all things 
hoped for have already been planned by God” and “God’s plan is the best”.  Participant 
E also said that “God is in control” and participant F said that it is “God’s way to do 
all these things”.  This belief helped them not to be anxious during the Covid-19 
pandemic.  See section VI.4.7 below for details of their responses to the pandemic. 
VI.4.5.3 Believe in heaven 
The belief in heaven helped to remove the fear of death and mitigate some of 
the fears of illnesses.  All the participants said that they were not afraid of death even 
though some said they have asked God to spare them from becoming bedridden before 
they die.  Both participants A and C believed that their husbands who passed away 
were in heaven.  When queried about her husband’s whereabout by her mother who 
had dementia, participant A told her: “Mum, he’s in heaven”.  When participant C 
talked about her late husband, she said: “I am sure he is with God”.    
Participant B also said: “I believe in God.  God said that you will be with me 
in heaven.  I already have the assurance that I will be with Him in heaven”.  Participant 
D believed that “the real beginning is in heaven” whereas participant F said: “Aiyo, 
death I go and meet my husband up there, my brother, then meet my Lord, the King. I 
want to go and see our Father in heaven. And God got mansion for us, we already 
said … our mansion is up there”.

God’s presence and either talked to Him or heard His call.  Four of the participants recounted

In her interview, participant D described one of the “miracles” that she experienced

forcing participant D to pray at the family altar.  This miracle made participant D thankful

VI.4.7 
Impact of Covid-19 
Because of my special interest in understanding how the pandemic had impacted 
the research participants, all the participants were asked about the impact of Covid-19 on 
them.  Their common response was that they were not fearful or anxious about the Covid-
19 pandemic.  Participant A said that she did not feel anxious about Covid-19 and her only 
regret is that she is not able visit the home that she volunteered at.  Participant B felt that 
“God is protecting us”, and participant C said that: “I was quite calm, I have peace, I am not 
worried and all that.  I know God will take care of that.  No anxiety or this and that.  I was 
quite calm. Whatever it is, God is in control.  So, leave it to Him, surrender to Him ah.” 
Participant D felt that “we are in the presence of God … you don’t need to worry about it”.  
Participant E said that he is “not worried” about it and participant F also said that there was 
no negative impact on her.   
Of the six, only participant D talked about Covid-19 being a wake-up call: 
“I just use spiritual thinking to think, I think people in this world keeps sinning, so God 
wants to use this thing to teach you restraint. So, everyone can't come out to meet 
up, ok?  They are locked up, have lots of quiet time to think”. 
The impact on their daily lives from the government-imposed pandemic-related 
restrictions ranged from feeling “very difficult” to “more happy”.  Participant C had to stop 
all her volunteering work so she felt that the Covid-19 restrictions had made her life “very

difficult” but was hopeful that the restrictions would be lifted once Covid-19 “stop”.  On the 
other hand, participant F said she was “more happy” because she could access Sunday 
worship services from other churches online.  
Overall, the participants’ perception of Covid-19 was affected by their faith 
positively.  They were not fearful or anxious and most of them expressed their belief that 
God is in control of the situation.

VI.5 Identifying Patterns/Themes 
Besides synthesizing the codes into categories to give them “consolidated meaning” per 
Saldaña (2016, pp.10),  I also noted that there were interactions between the categories.  These 
interactions or patterns emerged after the categories were created, especially after the last round 
of analysis using the Word tables.  By identifying the categories in the last column of the Word 
table for each section of the transcript, I could see the interaction between the identified categories 
and drew diagrams to illustrate such interactions.  These interactions or patterns were not only 
identified in the transcript of one participant but occurred across several participants.  This is 
explained in the following subsections.

VI.5.4 
Pattern of discerning 
In the pattern of indigenising, participants A and D integrated the local concepts of 
“kampung spirit” and “being filial” into their Christian living.  However, not all local 
concepts and practices were kept after the research participants became Christians.   
Participant B continued a local practice, i.e. social gambling, after he became a 
Christian, but eventually discarded it.  He said: “first few years I still play mahjong, after 
that I say: no no no, it's not the right way to become a Christian.  I give up everything, buy 
4D everything”.  4D is a form of lottery where one bets on which four-digit numbers would 
be the winning numbers published by 4D operators.  In the 70s, almost every village had 
runners from 4D syndicates that would collect bets from the villagers before a game day, 
usually weekends plus one more weekday.  Currently, 4D gambling is legal as long as it is 
organized by licensed operators.  Gambling using physical mahjong games is also legal when 
played among family and friends at home.  Such forms of “social gambling” are also 
acceptable and not frown upon among the locals.  However, participant B has chosen to 
disown this hobby.  He said: “the Lord guide me lah because before that, before that I was 
Taoist. I still gamble. I see people I go and gamble with him. I win his money, I feel I’m 
happy.  Before that I never think of that.  He earn a small salary, I go and gamble with him. 
Play mahjong all that, win his money, it’s not good. Now, I have empathy on people, I love 
people, I don’t want to hurt people, because he has a family to look after”.  Participant B 
mentioned two ways by which he decided whether to continue to own or to disown a practice: 
(i) based on his understanding of God’s will; and (ii) whether his heart is “at peace”.   In this

VII.1 Analysing the findings to answer my research question 
My research purpose was to understand how the spirituality of my research participants 
contributed towards their positive engagement with life.  What I found was that the selected 
research participants had their own theological beliefs which affected their dispositions and how 
they lived their daily lives including the spiritual practices that they engaged in.  These theological 
beliefs helped form their Christian worldview which allowed them to reframe their lived 
experiences positively.  They experienced healing and had the capacities to heal broken

were also able to be “at peace” and not be anxious about the vicissitudes of living as an OA in

In the Western literature of faith development much of the attention has been focused on

blessings in order to support the faith of other older Chrisitan adults in the Singaporean context.

with and difference from both Fowler and Westerhoff’s theories.

There are significant cultural critiques of Fowler’s work that suggested the Western 
origins of his theory obscure rather than illuminate how faith develops in non-Western 
contexts.  Fowler’s theory is heavily influenced by structural developmentalists Piaget and 
Kohlberg as well as Erikson’s work in psycho-social development (Fowler, 1995), all of 
whom have done their work in the Western context.  One key reason suggested by critics 
that Fowler’s theory might not be applicable outside the Western context was the material 
impact of the Enlightenment on his theory.  In Fowler’s own words: “the conception of faith 
with which faith development theory works is both made necessary by the Enlightenment 
and is part of the fruit of the Enlightenment’s effort to “reform” religion” (Fowler, 1992, 
p.25).  According to British practical theologian, Jane leach, in the West the Enlightenment 
emphasis on the turn to the subject and the demythologising of symbols into more abstract 
concepts has made this a stage that educated Christians need to go through to reach a new 
equilibrium in which story and metaphor find a new place.  In cultures that have not 
experienced the Enlightenment, the faith journey of individuals will inevitably be different 
(Leach, 1997).  This is consistent with findings of Asian research from India and Japan, that 
have shown that the developmental sequences followed in their countries are different from 
those in Western societies.  For instance, the Indian psychoanalyst, Kakar, in his study of the 
Indian childhood found that ego differentiation is “structurally weaker and comes 
chronologically later than in the West” (Kakar, 2004, p.104) because of a difference in “the 
pattern of their nurture” (Kakar, 2004, p.100).

Besides differences due to culture, another critique of Fowler’s theory is based on 
the feminist critique of Piaget.  Gilligan (Gilligan, 2016) and Slee (Slee, 2004) had pointed 
out that ego differentiation is much more relational in women even in the West as they are 
socialised into caring roles.  Since Stage IV relies on notions of individuation, women are

Based on my interaction with the local Chinese OAs, ego differentiation was also

with overseas Chinese had serious adverse consequences for the Chinese in mainland China.  
Many Chinese traditions and customs that were prohibited/discontinued during the CR 
because of the call to destroy the “old four”, i.e. old ideas, old culture, old customs, and old 
habits, were preserved by the Chinese in South-East Asia.  These included Confucian 
teachings and many Chinese folk religious practices. 
Singaporean Chinese OAs were strongly influenced by traditional Chinese teaching 
such as the Threefold obedience and Four virtues (i.e. San Cong Si De，三从四德).  The

Chinese traditions and practices, wrote: “the traditional Chinese practice of ancestral 
veneration is syncretic, with no clear line drawn between acts of reverence and respect, and 
acts of worship…Sound biblical discernment, therefore, must be exercised with regard to 
this practice to determine that which is acceptable to our Christian faith.  Rightly advocated 
and encouraged are the concepts of filial piety and social responsibility of mankind.  The 
family unit is truly something Christians must diligently work to maintain, uphold and build.  
Though ancestral worship is not to be practised by believers, we should nonetheless learn 
the need to remember our dearly departed with thanksgiving.  It is good for Christians to 
raise their children to remember their ancestors, as this would aid not only in the 
development of solidarity within the family, but also in the passing on (to the next generation) 
of the Christian faith” (Tong, 2012, p.113).  Hence, despite it being difficult to draw a line 
between showing respect and ancestor worship, it should still be drawn so that the baby is 
not thrown out with the water.  This is an especially sensitive issue because many Chinese 
families have family altars at home where tablets of deceased family members are placed 
and offerings are made regularly.  New Christians are likely to struggle to set boundaries 
when participating in such familial activities.

background, it is important to recognize that the contents of their faith had changed after

Shenism’s fundamental tenet is the belief in fate, that is, the belief that a person’s life in this 
world is predetermined – one cannot escape one’s fate. The key concern is to make the best

predetermined quota of ill-luck (Wee, 1976, pp.171, 172).  Hence, the content of faith for 
typical Shenists would include good luck as one of the centers of value, multiple “Shens” as 
images of power and an unchangeable life path as the master story.  This difference in faith 
contents should be taken into account when contextualising Christianity for new converts to 
transit smoothly into Christian living and it is a difficult journey when undertaken without 
help.  
The process of becoming Christian was not only one of discerning which key 
practices to discard and which to maintain.  For the research participants, the process of 
contextualising their faith was an ongoing personal challenge. None of the research 
participants talked about how their churches helped them in this process.  Fortunately, 
decades after becoming Christians, the research participants had contextualized their faith 
having the ability to own/disown selected local practices, live their faith within their social 
circles in an “at peace” state.

VII.1.3 Patterns of retelling and healing 
Under the theme of retelling (section VI.5.5), the research participants used their 
Christian worldview to reframe their lived experiences to arrive at a different understanding 
 
 
  
VERSION_DEC 2025 
 
 
185 
 
of such experiences.  This Christian worldview was built based on their theological beliefs.  
The ability to reframe their lived experiences allowed the research participants to integrate 
or re-integrate (if they attempted integration previously) the lived portion of their lives 
positively.  The term “integration” has a specific meaning in gerontology and it is used in 
this sense here.  It is the process of putting one’s life experiences into a coherent whole to 
see meaning/purpose in it (Mehta, 1997).   
VII.1.3.1 Reframing skills 
Reframing was a term used by the three founding members of brief therapy 
in their book on change.  According to Watzlawick, Weakland and Fisch, to reframe 
means to “change the conceptual and/or emotional setting or viewpoint in relation to 
which a situation is experienced and to place it in another frame which fits the “facts” 
of the same concrete situation equally well or even better, and thereby changes its 
entire meaning” (Watzlawick, Weakland and Fisch, 1974, p.65).  Such changes could 
be first-order change that occurs within a given system which itself remains unchanged; 
and second-order change that alters the system itself.  Both types of changes are useful 
but there are situations where first-order changes only create more of the same and are 
ineffective.  The American pastoral theologian, Donald Capps in his book “Reframing 
– A new method in pastoral care” showed how reframing was used by Jesus to bring 
about second-order changes.  He also identified 13 techniques of reframing (Capps, 
1990, pp.28–50) including dereflection, surrender tactic, providing a worst alternative, 
paradoxical intention, relabeling and benevolent sabotage.  These six named 
 
 
  
VERSION_DEC 2025 
 
 
186 
 
techniques were found in the interview transcripts with my research participants 
though none of the research participants mentioned that they were trained in such 
reframing.   The following are examples of these techniques used by the research 
participants: 
VII.1.3.1.1 Dereflection 
Dereflection is the reframing technique where one uses something else to 
detach from the problem to a more positive object (Capps, 1990, pp.30–31).  
Participant A was upset with her noisy neighbour (e.g. screaming in the night), after 
she had given them feedback about keeping the noise level down at night to no avail.  
Instead of “cursing” them or praying for them to be changed, she reframed the 
problem and asked for herself to be changed.  Participant A said: “I always say, 
give me the patience or give me… increase my focus on You so that I don’t focus 
more on this earth”. 
She shifted herself out of the worldly frame to focus on God, thus not 
allowing her peaceful way of life to be disturbed by her neighbours’ behaviour.  
This is a practical way of managing disputes with neighbours in Singapore because 
80% of the locals live in public housing flats, in close quarters with multiple 
neighbours.  Being embroiled in disputes with neighbours is not uncommon and 
can be highly disruptive to one’s life.  Moving away is normally not a good option 
because there are many regulations that make it difficult to sell and then buy another 
public housing flat.  Furthermore, one could be moving from one set of difficult 
 
 
  
VERSION_DEC 2025 
 
 
187 
 
neighbours to another set of neighbours who is just as difficult or even worse.  
Participant A’s ability to reframe not only is helpful in developing her relationship 
with God, it also improves her living standard by reducing the annoyance that she 
felt and the potential danger that she may encounter as a female OA living alone 
with neighbours who might be offended if she complained against them.  
VII.1.3.1.2 Surrender tactic 
Participant B has a hearing problem and had prayed for healing for many 
years.  However, the healing had not materialised.  Instead of seeing this as God not 
caring for him, he decided that it must be serving a purpose that he did not 
understand; and surrendered the situation to God saying that he will wait for God.  
He said: “At times, I may think that why God you have forsaken me, why You don’t 
heal me?  You touch me You say: “heal”, it will be healed.  The Word is power.  So 
I think everything God does there is a purpose, so at least I can hear and pass the 
day, then I am ok.  Wait till the Lord say: “my son, you will be healed”. 
VII.1.3.1.3 Providing a worst alternative 
Participant C was to be given away as an infant but her elder sister hid her 
from her prospective adoptive parents and brought her up.  This was how she 
reframed the event:  “I was about to be given away.  The couple came and take

VII.1.3.2 Reframing and healing 
The frame that one uses to interpret an event affects the conclusion drawn. 
Being able to reframe to see situations from a Christian worldview allowed the 
research participants to draw conclusions that are coherent with their Christian faith 
and created space for healing to take place.   For example, participant C became 
thankful and felt blessed instead of feeling upset with her parents for wanting to give 
her away.  Participant D was able to forgive her mother for her abuses and saw them 
as opportunities to “become stronger” and to receive God’s help.  Reframing helped 
them to heal their relationships with their parents.  From the findings, reframing did 
not seem difficult since all research participants were able to do it.  In fact, participant 
F demonstrated that she could reframe the Christian story for the Singaporean context 
where Christianity is not the dominant faith.  As such, she demonstrated some 
characteristics of Fowler’s Stage V – being alive to paradox and the truth in apparent 
contradictions, and striving to unify opposites in mind and experience. 
VII.2 The importance of the state of being “at peace”  
VII.2.1 The state of being “at peace” 
All the research participants talked about being at peace with God and themselves.  
Most of them went through suffering in their earlier years, including mistreatment by their 
own families.  Those who had broken relationships especially with their mothers, were able 
to heal those broken relationships as they lived their Christian faith.  One reason was their 
ability to reframe their past experiences using their Christian worldview (see 
 
 
  
VERSION_DEC 2025 
 
 
191 
 
sectionVII.1.3.2 above).  This Christian worldview allowed them to keep their life purpose 
in mind when reviewing their lives, see themselves in God’s plan and accept their lives as 
lived.  This state of being “at peace” could be compared to the results of achieving ego 
integrity in Erikson’s 8th stage or the result of being able to manage dissonance in “owned 
faith” described by Westerhoff.  This state also reminds me of the result of “integration” in 
old age that Mehta discussed in her paper  “The effects of religion on subjective aging in 
Singapore: An interreligious comparison.” (Jianbin and Mehta, 2003).  She wrote that: ‘‘At 
advanced old age when life’s journey is ending, the need for integration increases’’ largely 
because of ‘‘the heightened awareness of the reality of death and the attendant search for 
meaning to one’s life’’.  She defined “integration” at this stage as a complex, 
multidimensional subjective process through which the individual connects various parts of 
his/her life into a meaningful whole.  From her research, she concluded that “religion can 
play an integrative role, which facilitates personal adjustment to the aging process”(Mehta, 
1997).  Following this line of thought, their Christian faith allowed the research participants 
to go through the integrative process to arrive at the state of being “at peace”.  But this state 
can be lost as participants A and B mentioned in their interviews.
