VII.4 Conclusion 
Referring to chapter VI and the discussion in this chapter, my observations with respect to 
their Christian spirituality, i.e. lived reality of their faith, showed that all the six research 
participants were at the “owned faith” stage.  They have a Christian worldview which allowed 
them to live positively in their old age.  Although 36 beliefs were talked about during our 
interviews (refer to Table 13 Own theological beliefs and their codes), the five key beliefs that all 
of them shared were the key pillars in this worldview.  Their ability to reframe their lives as parts 
of God’s plan is important as that gave meaning to their existence.  They also demonstrated the 
ability to reframe key events in their lives, especially losses and abuses, into necessary parts of 
their master stories.  Thus, allowing these sufferings to make sense to them.  Some of the research 
participants contextualised their faith to incorporate local concepts/practices instead of trying to 
 
 
  
VERSION_DEC 2025 
 
 
195 
 
de-culturing themselves once they became Christians.  At least one of the research participants 
also demonstrated the ability to embrace paradoxes – going beyond the duality of “right” or “wrong” 
and embracing multiple perspectives and being conscious of the limits of her understanding.  They 
were also able to maintain the state of being “at peace” through spiritual practices such as attending 
church services, praying, and holding on to key verses.  
Given my primary interest has been to support the spirituality of Christian older adults, I 
believe that the most significant findings of this study relate to the faith contents, practices and 
skills that belong to the “owned faith” of these participants.  In the next chapter I synthesize these 
findings as my contribution to knowledge and as a basis for my contribution to practice. 
 
 
 
 
  
VERSION_DEC 2025 
 
 
196 
 
VIII. 
Chapter eight – Contributions to knowledge and practice 
This research has revealed the contents of the research participants’ faith, the skills and 
practices that have kept them positively engaged with life.  This information will help in designing 
interventions for local OAs and the results of such interventions could stimulate more research 
interest and support for spiritual interventions for OAs.  
VIII.1 Contribution to knowledge 
VIII.1.1 A local model of faith development 
The six research participants did not fit neatly into any of the stages of Fowler’s 
faith development theory but there was enough evidence that they have “owned faith” 
theorised by Westerhoff.  They were at peace in this stage as they have skills and practices 
to help them do so: (1) they were able to find themselves in the Christian master story so that 
they are part of God’s plan and their existence has meaning; (2) they were able to discern 
and decide which local practices/concepts to disown or own in their Christian living; (3) they 
could also reframe their lived lives, i.e. review their lived experiences and see them as part 
of their master stories.  One of them also displayed the ability to embrace paradoxes and 
reframe her Christian master story.  For more details, please refer to Table 19: Skills 
demonstrated by the research participants in chapter VII.  These skills are organized and 
presented in the spiral figure below.  The spiral indicates the movement within the “owned 
faith” stage as different skills developed and are demonstrated.  The ability to find 
themselves in the Christian master story to make it their own is placed first because upon 
conversion, this was what the research participants did.  The ability to embrace paradoxes is 
 
 
  
VERSION_DEC 2025 
 
 
197 
 
placed last among the skills on the spiral because it is wisdom which, if it develops, tends to 
develop much later in life.   The straight arrow represents the faith journey and “conversion” 
is written in blue font to highlight it as an important point of this journey.  After conversion, 
the research participants started their transformation towards the target of developing 
“Universal love” as they seek to become more like Jesus. 
 
Figure 7: Skills demonstrated by research participants 
Universal 
love 
 
 
  
VERSION_DEC 2025 
 
 
198 
 
 
Although this finding arose from only six participants, it suggests a way of 
understanding the faith of local Chinese Christian OAs and could be tested in a larger 
population of such adults.  In addition, the concentration on the development of “owned faith” 
suggests that this model may be relevant to Christian converts in other contexts. 
VIII.1.2 Master stories and theological beliefs of the research participants 
As mentioned in the last chapter, the five key beliefs shared by all the research 
participants were sufficient to build a worldview that is positively engaged with life.  These 
beliefs are the key contents of their faith – showing what they valued, the power that they 
relied on and their master stories.  The latter are the stories that we tell ourselves and by 
which we interpret and respond to events that impinge on our lives.  They disclose the 
ultimate meanings of our lives (Fowler, 1995, p.277).  After conversion, the research 
participants were able to find themselves in God’s plan, giving meaning to their existence.  
This finding is consistent with Mehta’s finding that religion helps the individual make 
meaning with his/her lived life (Jianbin and Mehta, 2003).  The research participants are in 
their old age and have spent most of their lives, that means if the lives that they lived did not 
serve a purpose or the purpose is no longer valid, it is difficult for them to be positive about 
life.  By believing that there is a caring God who has planned for and been in control of their 
lives, helped the research participants integrate their lived experiences into a coherent life 
with purpose.  In addition, the belief that they are destined for a better place, i.e. heaven, 
gives them hope even as their bodies fail.   
 
 
  
VERSION_DEC 2025 
 
 
199 
 
The five key beliefs identified as being important to the six research participants 
are likely to be useful to other Christian OAs.  They can be used as the basis for OAs to 
explore their own theological beliefs, their worldviews, and how these beliefs and 
worldviews have impacted their outlook of life in old age.  In other contexts, core beliefs 
might vary from this but the idea of inviting people to explore, discuss and consciously own 
their beliefs could be useful in clarifying their worldviews and the incongruencies that could 
be making them negative about their lives.  
VIII.1.3 Owning and disowning local concepts and practices 
The research project has highlighted the gap in supporting new converts when they 
were contextualizing their newfound faith for daily living.  From the interviews, the research 
participants learnt to manage the owning/disowning of local practices and concepts on their 
own.  Although they gained greater understanding of their faith through such struggles as 
suggested by participant D, there is also a risk that some might give up and acquiesce or use 
inappropriate compromises when they encounter such challenges.  The local churches have 
been dealing with this issue of contextualization for many years but new converts continue 
to struggle with these same local concepts/practices such as being filial, and respect for 
ancestors (discussed in section VI.5.3 above) indicating a gap in communication and support 
here. 
After decades of living their faith, the litmus test that the research participants used 
for discerning what to own/disown is the state of being “at peace”.  From their descriptions, 
this “at peace” can be a state of tranquility/serenity or the result of a good relationship with 
 
 
  
VERSION_DEC 2025 
 
 
200 
 
God, referring to Arichea’s descriptions of how the term “peace” is used in the New 
Testament (Arichea, 1987, p.201).  More research is needed to understand this test better but 
it is useful to have identified the two states of being “at peace” that were important to these 
OAs.  Being “at peace” shielded them from negative emotions and could be useful to other 
OAs too.  This finding provides evidence to support further research on how to help people 
achieve this state including helping to navigate owning/disowning as culture shifts and 
changes. 
VIII.1.4 Spiritual practices of the research participants 
In the owning/disowning process, the research participants talked about using “at 
peace” state as a tool.  They also talked about the practices that give them peace, i.e. going 
to church and prayers, besides maintaining a “trust and obey” attitude towards God (see 
section VII.2.2).  In discussing their spiritual experiences, praying was their main way of 
encountering God.  These spiritual experiences which included encounters with Jesus and 
answered prayers, were important in cementing the participants’ faith.   
Further, referring to the table of their spiritual practices, the five that were practiced 
by all research participants were: attending church and small group, praying, “quiet time” 
with God and studying the Bible (see Table 12).  For the research participants, “quiet time” 
is a time slot set aside to pray, read the daily devotional such as “Our Daily Bread” and read 
the Bible.  Attending church and small groups also involved praying.  Hence, the key 
spiritual practice for the research participants is praying.  Meditation is notably missing from 
table 12.  This is an area that could be explored for OAs to create more spaces for them to 
 
 
  
VERSION_DEC 2025 
 
 
201 
 
be in God’s presence.  This is to put oneself in the way of spiritual experiences and being 
formed by God’s universal love, and is different from skills and tasks.  It would involve 
increasing the range of spiritual practices. 
VIII.2 Contribution to practice 
VIII.2.1 Targeted Christian education for Christian OAs 
At the time of the interviews, I noted that the research participants attended small 
group meetings (see Table 12) regularly but most did not attend other Christian classes even 
before the Covid-19 pandemic.  The participants’ small groups met regularly for worship, 
discipleship, fellowship and outreach (except during part of the pandemic).  Because of the 
myriad of purposes, their small group meetings were not suitable for targeted education.  
From the findings of this project, classes to develop the skillsets shown in Figure 7 above 
would be helpful to OAs.  For example, reframing techniques can also be taught to those 
who have not reviewed their lived lives and are struggling to find meaning in them.  Each 
OA can be assessed so that they can be organized into appropriate groups according to their 
faith maturity, literacy and skills.  This would include compiling a list of their existing 
skillsets so that, for instance, they do not attend training in reframing if they are already 
trained in that.  This targeted education can include helping the Christian OAs develop their 
Christian worldview.  The findings from this research had shown that five key beliefs were 
sufficient to form the foundation of the research participants’ Christian worldview. These 
could be used as the baseline for discussion with OAs.  The OAs will be encouraged to 
explore their own beliefs and worldviews as well as their impact on the OAs’ outlook of life.  
 
 
  
VERSION_DEC 2025 
 
 
202 
 
For my own practice, I plan to work with my church to organize classes for 
Christian OAs who are interested in understanding their faith development.  The objective 
of these classes is to help the participants self-assess and reflect on their faith journeys to 
learn where they are and where they want to be on their faith journey.  Using the list of 
beliefs found in this project, the attendees will be encouraged to discuss their theological 
beliefs and how these have changed their daily living including their dispositions, the work 
they do and their spiritual practices etc.  They will also be encouraged to share any spiritual 
experiences that they may have encountered.  Again, the findings of this project will be 
useful for such classes – the spiritual experiences can be shared after anonymization to 
initiate discussions.  The theories of faith development from Westerhoff and Fowler will be 
discussed  as an option for those classes where the attendees are attuned to Western culture.  
Otherwise, only the model in Figure 7 will be discussed.  Hence the classes will be organized 
by their educational background, besides by cohort and language used.  They will be kept 
small, i.e. maximum five persons per class, to facilitate sharing and ensure confidentiality is 
preserved.  The information shared in the classes will be collected to aid my future research 
with the written consent of the attendees.  Each attendee will also have a one-to-one session 
so that the attendees will have customized maps of their faith development journeys and 
destinations.  They will also be introduced to reframing so that they can decide if they want 
to attend training in reframing.  As discussed in section VII.1.3, there are many reframing 
techniques available and they are not difficult to use as demonstrated by the six research 
participants in this project.  The purpose of the reframing training is to teach the OAs 
reframing techniques.  They will be encouraged to share one past event or an issue that has 
 
 
  
VERSION_DEC 2025 
 
 
203 
 
significantly impacted them so that the session is grounded in their lived experiences.  The 
reframing class will also be interactive because the attendees could have their own reframing 
techniques which I can learn from. 
VIII.2.2 Greater emphasis on increasing spiritual experiences 
Currently, members in my church are divided into small groups called “Life 
groups”.   A Life Group acts as a Bible study and fellowship group, and is the primary place 
of care and ministry as they are the “basic building blocks” of the church.  There are life 
groups for OAs but OAs are also found in other life groups such as those for ladies or couples 
etc.  This is because having members organized into small groups is the tradition of my 
church so many members grew old in their groups.   
Starting with the OA life groups, there could be greater emphasis on creating space 
for spiritual encounters with God.  The research participants said they had daily quiet time 
with God and most were able to pray anywhere and anytime.  Hence, they had more 
opportunities for spiritual encounters.  Bearing in mind this finding, there should be greater 
emphasis on waiting upon God as spiritual experiences had strengthen the research 
participants’ faith.  Together with the research results, I plan to research and organize a pack 
for these OA life groups emphasizing prayer as the space to encounter God.  This is not to 
de-emphasise the needs and prayer requests of these OAs but to encourage them to look at 
prayer from a different perspective, i.e. as a chance to wait upon God and sense His presence.  
Another practice notably missing from my research participants’ table of spiritual practices 
 
 
  
VERSION_DEC 2025 
 
 
204 
 
is meditation.  This is the other area that I plan to research into to increase spiritual 
encounters with God.  
VIII.2.3 Publicising Biblical understanding of local practices 
As mentioned earlier in section VII.1.2 above, the participants had struggled with 
owning/disowning local practices on their own.  The theological interpretation of local 
practices is important to help the local Christians contextualise their faith, there should also 
be practical implementation guides (e.g. Christian living in a Shenist family) that are 
acceptable to the local community to avoid any unnecessary guilt or fear on the part of the 
new Christian and misunderstanding or misgiving on the part of their non-Christian family 
and friends.  For my own practice, I plan to compile a booklet of those local practices that 
have been discussed in published books or articles with their theological interpretation and 
implementation guide for discussion with the church pastoral team.  The purpose is to get 
the church’s approval so that these can be shared with selected OA life groups.  There could 
be a pilot project to get feedback on their experiences with these practices.  A report will be 
submitted to the pastoral team after the pilot and further research or follow-up will be 
discussed with the team.   
VIII.3 Learning journey for me 
As mentioned in chapter II, this project has been transformational for me.  Not only did I 
learnt how to create space for God to show His grace e.g. through prayer times, more importantly, 
this project has changed my outlook towards living.  I used to feel guilty when I am not actively 
“doing” something but my health did not permit a busy lifestyle now, hence I go through emotional 
 
 
  
VERSION_DEC 2025 
 
 
205 
 
cycles of ups and downs.  I was also “triggered” by the health and care issues raised by the OAs 
that I worked with.  Having learnt how the research participants lived their core beliefs, I examined 
my own core beliefs and realized that I have not been living them.  This realisation made it possible 
for me to truly accept that I am part of God’s plan and need to flow with it.  With my new slogan 
of “accept and flow”, I have been able to manage the recent bouts of illnesses/hospitalisation 
without big mood swings.  Although there was sadness, there was also thankfulness because I 
know where we are heading.  Hence, for me, this learning journey has been valuable and necessary.   
VIII.4 Limitations and Future Research 
The research participants for this project were Chinese Christian OAs who were selected 
because they were positively engaged with life.  Because of these very specific selection criteria, 
the sample was not representative of the larger population of OAs nor of the Christians of 
Singapore.  It  describes the experience of a specific aging cohort.  Later cohorts will have had a 
different social and educational experience and this may shape their spiritual experiences and 
needs differently.  Hence, the findings of this study cannot be generalized. These findings are 
useful though for the study of other cohorts, such as those of other age ranges, other ethnicities 
and religions.  The next project that I would like to undertake would be to understand the 
spirituality of Christian Indian-Singaporean OAs who are positively engaged with life.  The 
Indians are the next largest group after Chinese among the local Christians.  Many of them are also 
converts like the Chinese OAs.  Going forward, with appropriate support and funding, future 
research projects on Muslim OAs, Buddhist/Taoist OAs and those OAs without professed religions 
would allow local gerontologists to have a better understanding of the diverse spirituality of local 
 
 
  
VERSION_DEC 2025 
 
 
206 
 
OAs and how to support their spiritual needs effectively in old age.  In addition to using the 
methodology from this study to study other cohorts, a further area of research concerns the way in 
which the state of “being at peace” functioned for this cohort.  I am interested to know how 
culturally specific this criterion is for Christian discernment, and to know more about how it is 
experienced and used as a principle for integrating faith and life.    
VIII.5 Conclusion 
Through this research project, pictures of the lived reality of the research participants’ faith 
were revealed allowing an assessment of how it supported them in old age.  Their faith stages were 
also compared against seminal theories of Fowler and Westerhoff to determine their applicability 
to the research participants. Westerhoff’s theory was a better fit because Fowler’s theory is more 
embedded in the Western culture as compared to Westerhoff.  Notwithstanding, both aided in 
developing a model of faith which I have trialed with a fellow Christian who found it to be 
enlightening for her.  Given that the research design, the findings is not generalizable to a larger 
population but the project has met its research purpose and highlighted areas for fruitful research 
e.g. the state of being “at peace”, its implication for OAs and how it could be attained; whether a 
similar state of spirituality would support Christian OAs of other ethnicities etc.  Furthermore, the 
findings will be used to develop customized classes and information packs for OAs that I work 
with.  The feedback and lessons learnt from these will be compiled and used to generate questions 
for my future research with other ethnic and faith groups.
